content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Tom Schroeter 
City Attorney 
City of Tehachapi 
254 H Street 
Bakersfield, CA 93304 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-006 
Dear Mr. Schroeter: 
February 19, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090. 1 Please note that we are only providing 
advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not under other general 
conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying· these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kem County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )(5).) 
The Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b)(8)(A).) Accordingly, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any past 
conduct, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. 
QUESTIONS 
Would Section 1090 or the Act prohibit the Councilmember, who operates a business on 
Tehachapi Municipal Airport land, owned by the City of Tehachapi, from taking part in the "design 
grant phase" in which the City would seek bids and ultimately adopt a formal resolution regarding 
repairs that would bring the airport into compliance with federal standards? If prohibited under 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FileNo. A-19-006 
Page No. 2 
Section 1090, may the City apply for and receive a grant from the Federal Aviation Administration 
("FAA") if the Councilmember does not take part in the decisions? 
CONCLUSION 
The Councilmember would be financially interested in the grant under Section 1090 and 
may not take part in decisions regarding the grant. However, the "rule of necessity" would allow the 
City to take part in the contracting process in order to ensure that the Tehachapi Municipal Airport 
continues to operate safely. Because the financially interested Councilmember must abstain under 
Section 1090, we do not further analyze the potential conflict of interest issue under the Political 
Reform Act, but note that the Act would require the Councilmember to announce his interest and 
leave the room when recusing himself from the decision. (See Regulation 18707.) 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY THE REQUESTOR 
The City of Tehachapi owns and operates the Tehachapi Municipal Airport. One 
Councilmember leases airport land from the City. These leases are set to expire in 2023. For over 
ten years, the Councilmember and his wife have owned and operated a business on the airport ("the 
business"). They are the business's only employees. They manage the business together and have 
invested more than $2,000 in the business. The business owns six hangars at the airport on land 
leased from the City. Four of the hangars have been subleased to other individuals. In the remaining 
two hangars, the business provides aircraft rental and maintenance services. 
The airport's "Taxiway A" is currently not in compliance with FAA design standards. The 
FAA has requested that the City bring the Taxiway into the compliance with federal standards, but 
the FAA has not established a deadline for these improvements. At its December 1 7, 2018 meeting, 
the potentially interested councilmember recused himself and the remaining City Council voted to 
authorize staff to submit a "pre-application" for an FAA design grant for improvements related to 
Taxiway A, as well as other important upgrades. This includes constructing Taxiway A to meet 
FAA standards and fix deteriorating pavements along the taxiways. The project will also address 
airfield drainage and erosion problems. 
The current funding (pre-application) supports only the design phase. If the City's pre-
application is tentatively approved by the FAA, staff will prepare the project to a "bid ready" stage 
and solicit bids. Once bids are received and presuming they fall within the allocated budget, staff 
will return to the City Council to request a formal resolution supporting the project. This will be 
followed by a full application to the FAA for a grant to pay for the design. In future years, after 
completion of the design phase, the City will likely submit a separate application for additional 
FAA funding to complete the construction phase of the project. 
The City recently issued a draft environmental assessment for the project. The public 
comment for the draft environmental assessment closed on November 26, 2018. According to a 
comment letter received from the Hangar Owners Association (of which the Councilmember is a 
member), the Councilmember's business will be financially affected "radically more" than other 
aircraft owners. The construction of the project may impact access between the airport's runway 
and the hangars owned by the business. During construction, airplanes leaving the hangars owned 


File No. A-19-006 
Page No. 3 
by the business may be diverted south towards a diagonal taxiway that is a less direct route from the 
hangars to the runway than by way of Taxiway A. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 provides that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,judicial 
district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by 
them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." "The 
prohibition is based on the rationale that a person cannot effectively serve two masters at the same 
time." (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 298.) Section 1090 
applies to officials who participate in any way in making of the contract, including involvement in 
matters such as preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing 
of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City 
of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) A 
contract that violates Section 1090 is void, and the prohibition applies regardless of whether the 
terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (See Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 
633, 646.) Grants are generally considered contracts for Section 1090 purposes. (See People v. 
Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 349-53 [rejecting a claim that a grant was not a contract within 
the meaning of Section 1090].) 
Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General opinions indicate that the term is to be liberally interpreted. The prohibited 
financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the 
possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. "The defining characteristic of a 
prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and 
compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with 
protecting." (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1075.) "Thus, that the interest 'might 
be small or indirect is immaterial so long as it is such as deprives the [people] of his overriding 
fidelity to [them] and places him in the compromising situation where, in the exercise of his official 
judgment or discretion, he may be influenced by personal considerations rather than the public 
good.'" (Ibid) 
Here, the Councilmember owns a business that operates on land leased from the City. The 
leases are set to expire in 2023. The Councilmember has more than $2,000 invested in the business, 
which owns six hangars located on the leased property. According to a comment from the Hangar 
Owners Association, the Councilmember's business would be financially affected "radically more" 
than other aircraft owners by the construction, as it might impact access between the airport's 
runway and the hangars owned by the business and could require that airplanes leaving those 
hangars to be diverted toward an alternate taxiway. 
The above circumstances indicate financial interests in the foreseeable contracts related to 
the airport renovations, including the potential design and construction grants from the FAA, as 
well as contracts with companies to perform the repairs. In the short-run, the service that the 
Councilmember's business provides could be negatively impacted by renovations that would make 


File No. A-19-006 
Page No. 4 
the hangars less easily accessible. In the long-run, the business could be positively impacted, 
because the airport land would be improved and in compliance with FAA standards (presumably 
affecting the airport's ability to continue operating without penalty). For the same reasons, the value 
of the Councilmember's leasehold interest(s) would also be affected by potential contracts to 
renovate the airport land. Accordingly, Section I 090 would prohibit the Councilmember from 
taking part in any contracts related to the project that would implicate his financial interests in his 
business (and source-of-income), as well as his leasehold interest(s). 
An officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's 
contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the 
contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, at p. 649.) As a general rule, when Section I 090 is applicable to 
one member of a governing body of a public entity, the entire governing body is precluded from 
entering into the contract. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) 
The "Public Services" Non-Interest Exception Under Section 1091. 5 (a)(3) 
None of the statutory exceptions to Section 1090 are relevant here. You have specifically 
inquired regarding the potential applicability of Section 1091.5(a)(3), which states that ''[a]n officer 
or employee shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract if his or her interest is ... "[t]hat of a 
recipient of public services generally provided by the public body or board of which he or she is a 
member, on the same terms and conditions as ifhe or she was not a member of the body or board." 
Attorney General opinions and case law make clear that the exception is intended to apply only to 
services that are uniformly provided to all customers and for which rates and charges have been 
clearly established, such as public utilities (such as water, gas, and electricity), and the renting of 
hangar space in a municipal airport on a first come, first served basis. (See, e.g., 81 
Ops.Cal.Atty .Gen. at p. 319; City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 
Cal.App.4th 50, 514-15.) 
The phrase "on the same terms and conditions" requires there be no special treatment of an 
official, either express or implied, because of that person's status as an official. (Lexin, supra, 47 
Cal.4th at p. 1101.) Accordingly, the public services exception generally will not apply when the 
provision of the services involves an exercise of discretion by the public body that would allow 
favoritism toward officials, or occurs on terms tailored to an official's particular circumstances. 
Here, the City Council will be considering and ultimately selecting at least one bid involving 
renovations that may have a disproportionate effect on the Councilmember, who subleases four 
hangars and operates a business out of two others. Because the discretionary decision of the Council 
in selecting a bid would allow favoritism toward the Councilmember, or could occur on terms 
tailored to the Councilmember' s particular circumstances, the public services exception does not 
apply here. 
The Rule of Necessity 
In limited circumstances, the "rule of necessity" allows a contract otherwise violating 
Section 1090 to ensure that essential government functions are performed. (69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
102, 109 (1986).) In this case, Tehachapi Municipal Airport is a city-owned airport and Taxiway A 
is currently not in compliance with federal standards, and has deteriorating pavement, as well as 


File No. A-19-006 
Page No. 5 
airfield drainage and erosion problems. Presumably, in the absence of the planned renovations, use 
of the airport would become increasingly dangerous. Thus, the grants/contracts related to repairing 
the airport so that it complies with FAA standards are essential to the operation of the City's airport. 
Accordingly, we find that the rule of necessity applies and permits the City to contract for necessary 
improvements for the sake of public safety. The interested Councilmember, however, must abstain 
from any participation in the decision. 
The Political Reform Act 
Because the Co~cilmember must abstain from taking part in the contracts related to the 
airport repairs, we do not need to further analyze the potential conflict of interest issue under the 
Act. By following the abstention requirements of Section 1090 and additionally leaving the room 
during the decisions, the Councilmember would satisfy the Act's recusal provisions set forth in 
Section 87105 and Regulation 18707. As such, we do not analyze the Act's conflict of interest 
provisions further. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

Section 1090

district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by
